details.architect: Skidmore, Owings and Merrill; Bruce Graham and Fazlur Rahman Khan
details.gloss: The John Hancock Center exposes its X-bracing as both structure and image. Its mixed-use height helped redefine the expressive possibilities of the skyscraper.

image.alt: Architectural view of Transamerica Pyramid.
image.license: Free license or public domain; see Wikimedia Commons file page

details.gloss: The Transamerica Pyramid turned a tapered office tower into San Francisco's most recognizable skyline marker. Its shape balanced structural logic with bold corporate identity.

image.alt: Architectural view of Kimbell Art Museum.
image.license: Free license or public domain; see Wikimedia Commons file page

details.gloss: Kahn's Kimbell uses cycloid vaults, controlled daylight, and measured proportions to create calm galleries. It is a benchmark for museum architecture.

image.alt: Architectural view of Glass House.
image.license: Free license or public domain; see Wikimedia Commons file page

details.gloss: The Glass House reduces domestic architecture to a transparent box of steel, glass, and landscape. It became a touchstone of American modernist experimentation.

image.alt: Architectural view of Eames House.
image.license: Free license or public domain; see Wikimedia Commons file page

details.gloss: The Eames House used prefabricated steel parts to create a flexible home and studio. Its color, collage-like facade, and lived-in interior made modernism humane.

image.alt: Architectural view of Hearst Tower.
image.license: Free license or public domain; see Wikimedia Commons file page

details.gloss: Hearst Tower lifts a faceted diagrid office tower above a preserved 1920s base. Its structure and environmental systems made it a high-tech Manhattan landmark.

image.alt: Architectural view of Milwaukee Art Museum.
image.license: Free license or public domain; see Wikimedia Commons file page

details.architect: Santiago Calatrava; earlier additions by Eero Saarinen and David Kahler
details.gloss: Calatrava's movable wing-like sunscreen turned the Milwaukee Art Museum into a lakeside icon. Its kinetic form made the museum instantly recognizable.

image.alt: Architectural view of Turning Torso.
image.license: Free license or public domain; see Wikimedia Commons file page

details.gloss: Turning Torso translates a twisting human figure into stacked tower segments. It became a symbol of Malmo's waterfront redevelopment and Scandinavian high-rise ambition.

image.alt: Architectural view of Metropol Parasol.
image.license: Free license or public domain; see Wikimedia Commons file page

details.gloss: Metropol Parasol covers a city square with a vast timber lattice of walkways and shade. Its scale and texture turned an archaeological site into a public landmark.

image.alt: Architectural view of Oslo Opera House.
image.license: Free license or public domain; see Wikimedia Commons file page

details.gloss: Oslo Opera House invites the public to walk over its sloping marble roof to the harbor edge. It made cultural architecture part of the city's outdoor life.

image.alt: Architectural view of Harpa.
image.license: Free license or public domain; see Wikimedia Commons file page

details.architect: Henning Larsen Architects and Batteríið Architects; facade by Olafur Eliasson
details.gloss: Harpa's crystalline glass facade catches Icelandic light and harbor views. The concert hall became a civic symbol after opening during economic recovery.

image.alt: Architectural view of Museum of Islamic Art, Doha.
image.license: Free license or public domain; see Wikimedia Commons file page

details.gloss: Pei's museum abstracts Islamic geometric traditions into limestone volumes on the Doha waterfront. It gave Qatar a powerful cultural landmark.

image.alt: Architectural view of Louvre Abu Dhabi.
image.license: Free license or public domain; see Wikimedia Commons file page

details.gloss: Nouvel's museum shelters galleries beneath a vast perforated dome that filters sun into a rain of light. It became Abu Dhabi's signature cultural building.

image.alt: Architectural view of National Museum of Qatar.
image.license: Free license or public domain; see Wikimedia Commons file page

details.gloss: The museum's interlocking disks evoke a desert rose while wrapping around an older palace. It turns national storytelling into a dramatic contemporary landmark.

title: National Centre for the Performing Arts

image.alt: Architectural view of National Centre for the Performing Arts.
image.license: Free license or public domain; see Wikimedia Commons file page

details.gloss: The Beijing performing arts center places theaters inside a smooth ellipsoid dome surrounded by water. Its futuristic form contrasts sharply with nearby historic axes.

image.alt: Architectural view of Apple Park.
image.license: Free license or public domain; see Wikimedia Commons file page

details.gloss: Apple Park turns a corporate campus into a giant circular glass ring set in a carefully landscaped park. Its scale and secrecy made it a 21st-century tech icon.

image.alt: Hermann Thiersch reconstruction drawing of the Lighthouse of Alexandria.

details.constructionDates: Built c. 280 BC; destroyed by earthquakes between AD 956 and 1323
details.gloss: The Pharos guided ships into Alexandria with a huge tower on the harbor island. As a Seven Wonder and prototype lighthouse, it made engineering and navigation visible at monumental scale.

image.alt: Nineteenth-century imagined view of the ancient Library of Alexandria.

details.constructionDates: Founded c. 3rd century BC; lost in late antiquity
details.gloss: The Library of Alexandria was the scholarly heart of the Ptolemaic capital. Though its exact buildings are lost, the Mouseion-library complex became the emblem of ancient learning.

image.alt: Scale model of the Temple of Artemis at Ephesus.
image.license: Free license or public domain; see Wikimedia Commons file page

details.architect: Chersiphron and Metagenes; later rebuilt by Dinocrates, traditionally credited
details.constructionDates: Built c. 550 BC; rebuilt after the 356 BC fire; later destroyed
details.gloss: The Ephesian temple turned Artemis's sanctuary into one of antiquity's great marble monuments. Rebuilt after fires and raids, its lost colonnades made it a Seven Wonder.

image.alt: Museum model reconstructing the Mausoleum at Halicarnassus.
image.license: Free license or public domain; see Wikimedia Commons file page

details.architect: Satyros and Pythius of Priene, traditionally credited
details.constructionDates: Built c. 353-351 BC; ruined by earthquakes in the Middle Ages
details.gloss: The tomb of Mausolus gave its name to all later mausoleums. Its high podium, Ionic colonnade, and sculptural program made dynastic burial into a Seven Wonder.

image.alt: Historic imagined view of the Hanging Gardens of Babylon.
image.license: Free license or public domain; see Wikimedia Commons file page

details.architect: Unknown; traditionally attributed to Nebuchadnezzar II
details.constructionDates: Legendary; traditionally dated c. 6th century BC
details.gloss: The Hanging Gardens survive as a contested ancient wonder: terraced greenery raised above a Mesopotamian city. Their uncertain location is part of their architectural memory.

image.alt: Model reconstruction of Jerusalem's Second Temple precinct.

details.architect: Zerubbabel's builders; Herod the Great expanded the precinct
details.constructionDates: Built c. 516 BC; Herodian expansion from c. 20 BC; destroyed AD 70
details.gloss: The Second Temple rebuilt Jerusalem's sacred center and later grew under Herod into a vast platform and sanctuary. Its destruction in AD 70 reshaped Jewish history.

image.alt: Ruined columns and stone platform of the Apadana at Persepolis.

details.architect: Royal builders of Darius I and Xerxes I
details.constructionDates: Built c. 515-465 BC; burned in 330 BC
details.gloss: The Apadana was Persepolis's great audience hall, raised for Achaemenid ceremony. Its stair reliefs turn architecture into a procession of subject peoples and royal power.

image.alt: Ruins of European-style stone buildings at the Old Summer Palace in Beijing.
image.license: Free license or public domain; see Wikimedia Commons file page

details.architect: Qing imperial workshops and court designers; Giuseppe Castiglione contributed to the Western mansions
details.constructionDates: Built from 1707; looted and burned in 1860
details.gloss: Yuanmingyuan fused Chinese garden design with imperial palaces and European-style pavilions. Its 1860 destruction made the ruins a powerful symbol of cultural loss.

image.alt: Historic exterior image of the iron-and-glass Crystal Palace.
image.license: Free license or public domain; see Wikimedia Commons file page

details.constructionDates: Built in 1851; moved in 1854; destroyed by fire in 1936
details.gloss: The Crystal Palace made iron and glass architecture a public spectacle for the Great Exhibition. Its prefabricated transparency foreshadowed later modern building systems.

image.alt: Aerial view of the original Pennsylvania Station in New York City in the 1910s.

details.gloss: Original Penn Station brought Roman bath scale to a rail terminal of granite, glass, and steel. Its demolition became a defining catalyst for preservation in New York.

image.alt: Frank Lloyd Wright's Imperial Hotel in Tokyo before demolition.
image.license: Free license or public domain; see Wikimedia Commons file page

details.gloss: Wright's Imperial Hotel fused modern structure with Maya-inspired ornament and Japanese craft. Its survival of the 1923 earthquake became part of its architectural legend.

image.alt: The twin towers of the original World Trade Center in New York City.

details.architect: Minoru Yamasaki with Emery Roth and Sons
details.gloss: Yamasaki's twin towers made the office skyscraper into a pair of abstract vertical icons. Their destruction in 2001 turned the complex into a central site of modern memory.

image.alt: The pink Italianate-Eclectic facade of Casa Rosada in Buenos Aires.

details.architect: Carl Kihlberg, Henrik Aberg, and Francesco Tamburini
details.gloss: Casa Rosada joins a former post office and government house into Argentina's pink presidential seat. Its Italianate-Eclectic facade anchors Plaza de Mayo's political stage.
